title: Steady, steady, steady
subtitle: A short repeated affirmation for the days the feast-and-famine swing is loud.
excerpt: A short repeated affirmation for the days when money runs in swings: a flush week followed by a tight one, a windfall followed by a shortfall. Three words, said three times, hand on the centre of the chest. The repetition is the practice.
sourceNotes: Adapted from Day 4 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "End the feast-and-famine cycle". The affirmation shape (present-tense, positive construction, repetition) follows public-domain affirmation craft, with roots in the New Thought movement of the late 19th century and modern-form popularisation through writers like Louise Hay (You Can Heal Your Life, 1984).

content.text: A short affirmation for the days money runs in swings: a flush week followed by a tight one, a windfall followed by a shortfall. Three words, said three times, hand on the centre of the chest. The work is the steadiness the repetition wears in over weeks.

Money sometimes runs in swings. A week where the bank balance is fuller than usual, then a week where it is tighter. A payment that lands, then weeks before the next one. The body learns to brace for both ends: for the loss that comes after the win, for the shortfall that comes after the flush. The affirmation is the counter-pattern. One word, said three times, asking the body to hold steady rather than swing.
Three times, slow. Hand flat on the centre of the chest, palm warm against the breastbone. Say it out loud if the room allows it, in your mind if it does not. Each word lands fully before the next.

Use it at the swing moments: when a payment lands and the impulse is to spend it before it leaves, when the balance dips and the chest tightens, before opening the banking app, after a hard money conversation. Three repetitions at each moment. No more is needed.
Stay with this one sentence for the full week. Switching to a different affirmation each day stops any of them from landing; the wearing-in is the point.
If the chest is too loud for the words to land, tap first.
covers the morning version. For a midday reset,
I am safe and steady with money today

Adapted from Day 4 of MONEY (Rebecca J Page, 2025), where this affirmation closes the work on the feast-and-famine cycle. The form (present tense, positive, repeated daily over weeks) follows public-domain affirmation craft. Its roots are in the New Thought movement of the late 1800s, brought into modern form by writers like Louise Hay.
